questions.text: The unit of work is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Joule**

Explanation:
Work in physics is defined as force multiplied by displacement in the direction of force.

Work = Force × Distance

Option Analysis:
• Joule → SI unit of work and energy.
• Neutron → A subatomic particle, not a unit.
• Watt → Unit of power (rate of doing work).
• Dyne → CGS unit of force.

Exam Insight:
1 Joule = 1 Newton × 1 metre.

questions.text: The SI unit of electrical resistance of conductor is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Ohm-metre**

Explanation:
Electrical resistivity is measured in ohm-metre.

Option Analysis:
• Faraday → Unit related to electric charge in electrochemistry.
• Volt → Unit of potential difference.
• Ampere → Unit of electric current.
• Ohm-metre → Unit of electrical resistivity of materials.

questions.text: The unit of power is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Watt**

Explanation:
Power represents the rate of doing work.

Power = Work / Time

Option Analysis:
• Hertz → Unit of frequency.
• Volt → Electric potential difference.
• Watt → Correct SI unit of power.
• Neutron → Subatomic particle.

questions.text: The unit of the force is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Newton**

Explanation:
Force = mass × acceleration.

Option Analysis:
• Faraday → Unit of electric charge.
• Fermi → Unit of nuclear distance.
• Newton → SI unit of force.
• Rutherford → Nuclear physics unit.

questions.text: Light year is the unit of
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Distance**

Explanation:
A light year is the distance travelled by light in one year.

1 light year ≈ 9.46 × 10¹² km.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the unit of measure of the thickness of the ozone layer of the atmosphere?
questions.solution:
This question relates to **atmospheric science and environmental chemistry**, which occasionally appears in the General Science section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Which one of the following is the unit of measure of the thickness of the ozone layer of the atmosphere?**

A. Knot
B. Dobson
C. Poise
D. Maxwell

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Dobson**

---

# Core Concept

The **thickness (amount) of ozone in the atmosphere** is measured in **Dobson Units (DU)**.

The unit is named after **Gordon M. B. Dobson**, who developed instruments to measure ozone in the atmosphere.

---

# What is a Dobson Unit?

**1 Dobson Unit (DU)** represents:

[
0.01 \text{ mm thickness of pure ozone at standard temperature and pressure}
]

Example:

If all ozone in a vertical column of atmosphere were compressed at sea level:

• **300 DU ≈ 3 mm thick ozone layer**

Typical ozone layer thickness:

• Around **300 Dobson Units**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Knot ❌ (Incorrect)

A **knot** is a unit used to measure **speed**, especially:

• Speed of ships
• Wind speed in meteorology

[
1 \text{ knot} = 1.852 \text{ km/h}
]

Thus it has **nothing to do with ozone measurement**.

---

## B. Dobson ✔ (Correct)

A **Dobson Unit (DU)** measures the **total amount of ozone in the atmosphere**.

It is the standard unit used by scientists to monitor:

• **Ozone layer thickness**
• **Ozone depletion**

Thus this is the correct answer.

---

## C. Poise ❌ (Incorrect)

**Poise** is the unit of **dynamic viscosity** in the CGS system.

Example:

• Viscosity of liquids like oil or honey.

So it is unrelated to atmospheric ozone.

---

## D. Maxwell ❌ (Incorrect)

**Maxwell** is a unit used in **magnetism**.

It measures **magnetic flux** in the CGS system.

Thus it has no relation to ozone thickness.

---

# Quick Summary

| Unit | Measures |
|---|---|
| Dobson | Thickness of ozone layer |
| Knot | Speed |
| Poise | Viscosity |
| Maxwell | Magnetic flux |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

If the question mentions **ozone layer thickness**, the answer is almost always:

👉 **Dobson Unit (DU)**.

---

You can also review **important environmental science concepts such as ozone depletion, greenhouse gases, acid rain, and atmospheric pollutants**, which frequently appear in **DRDO CEPTAM and SSC examinations**. Environmental science questions appear quite often in the General Science part of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**. Usually they test **basic facts about ozone layer, greenhouse gases, acid rain, and pollution**.

Below is a **high-yield revision sheet**.

---

# 20 Important Environmental Science Facts

## 1. Ozone Layer

• Located in the **stratosphere (15–35 km above Earth)**.
• Protects Earth from **ultraviolet (UV) radiation**.

**Unit of measurement:**

• **Dobson Unit (DU)**

---

## 2. Ozone Depletion

Main cause:

• **CFCs (Chlorofluorocarbons)**

Examples of sources:

• Refrigerators
• Aerosol sprays
• Air conditioners

Important agreement:

• **Montreal Protocol (1987)**

---

# 3. Greenhouse Effect

The greenhouse effect is the **trapping of heat in Earth’s atmosphere**.

Main greenhouse gases:

| Gas | Source |
|---|---|
| Carbon dioxide (CO₂) | Burning fossil fuels |
| Methane (CH₄) | Cattle, wetlands |
| Nitrous oxide (N₂O) | Fertilizers |
| Water vapor | Natural evaporation |

Major contributor: **CO₂**

---

# 4. Global Warming

Increase in Earth's average temperature due to greenhouse gases.

Effects:

• Melting glaciers
• Sea level rise
• Climate change

---

# 5. Acid Rain

Acid rain forms when gases react with water in the atmosphere.

Main gases:

• **SO₂ (Sulfur dioxide)**
• **NO₂ (Nitrogen dioxide)**

Acids formed:

• Sulfuric acid
• Nitric acid

pH of acid rain:

[
< 5.6
]

---

# 6. Photochemical Smog

Occurs when:

• Sunlight reacts with **nitrogen oxides and hydrocarbons**

Main pollutant:

• **Ozone at ground level**

Common in:

• Large cities with heavy traffic

---

# 7. Eutrophication

Excess nutrients in water bodies cause:

• Rapid growth of algae
• Decrease in oxygen

Main cause:

• Fertilizer runoff

---

# 8. Biomagnification

Increase in concentration of toxic substances along the food chain.

Example chemical:

• **DDT**

---

# 9. BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)

Measures **amount of oxygen required by microorganisms** to decompose organic matter in water.

Higher BOD → **more polluted water**.

---

# 10. Noise Pollution

Measured in **decibels (dB)**.

Sources:

• Traffic
• Machinery
• Aircraft

---

# Very Important One-Line Facts

1. Ozone layer thickness → **Dobson Unit**
2. Ozone depletion gas → **CFC**
3. Acid rain gases → **SO₂ and NO₂**
4. Major greenhouse gas → **CO₂**
5. Noise pollution unit → **Decibel**
6. Water pollution indicator → **BOD**

---

# Example Exam MCQ

**Which gas is mainly responsible for ozone depletion?**

A. Carbon dioxide
B. Chlorofluorocarbons
C. Oxygen
D. Nitrogen

✅ **Answer: Chlorofluorocarbons**

---

You can also review **important one-line facts from Physics, Chemistry, and Biology**, which together cover a large portion of **DRDO CEPTAM Tier-1 General Science questions**.

questions.text: ‘Light Year’ is
options: The year in which February has 29 days
The distance travelled by light in one year
The time which Sun rays take to reach the Earth
The time in which a spacecraft reaches Moon from the Earth
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: The distance travelled by light in one year**

Explanation:
Light year is a distance unit used in astronomy.

questions.text: A parsec, a unit of distance used to measure the distance related to the stars in the sky, is equal to
questions.solution:
This question is from **astronomy / units of astronomical distance**, which occasionally appears in the Physics section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**A parsec, a unit of distance used to measure the distance related to stars in the sky, is equal to**

A. 4.25 light years
B. 3.25 light years
C. 4.50 light years
D. 3.05 light years

✅ **Correct Answer: B. 3.25 light years**
(Actual precise value ≈ **3.26 light years**, so 3.25 is the closest option.)

---

# What is a Parsec?

A **parsec (pc)** is a unit used in astronomy to measure **very large distances between stars and galaxies**.

It is defined based on **parallax measurement of stars**.

Definition:

A parsec is the **distance at which one astronomical unit subtends an angle of one arcsecond**.

---

# Conversion Values

| Unit | Equivalent Distance |
|---|---|
| 1 parsec | **≈ 3.26 light years** |
| 1 parsec | ≈ (3.086 × 10^{16}) meters |

Since **3.26 light years** is closest to **3.25 light years**, option **B** is correct.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. 4.25 light years ❌ (Incorrect)

This value is **too large** compared with the actual value of a parsec.

Actual value ≈ **3.26 light years**, not 4.25.

---

## B. 3.25 light years ✔ (Correct)

This is **closest to the correct value**.

[
1 \text{ parsec } \approx 3.26 \text{ light years}
]

Thus option **B** is correct.

---

## C. 4.50 light years ❌ (Incorrect)

This value is **much larger than the actual parsec value**, so it is incorrect.

---

## D. 3.05 light years ❌ (Incorrect)

This value is **smaller than the real value (3.26)**, so it is also incorrect.

---

# Important Astronomy Units

| Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Astronomical Unit (AU) | Distance between Earth and Sun |
| Light Year | Distance light travels in 1 year |
| Parsec | ≈ 3.26 light years |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember this relation:

[
1 \text{ parsec } \approx 3.26 \text{ light years}
]

If options are approximate, choose **3.25 light years**.

---

You can also review **important astronomy concepts such as astronomical unit (AU), light year, parsec, black holes, and galaxies**, which occasionally appear in **DRDO CEPTAM and SSC examinations**. Astronomy questions occasionally appear in the General Science section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**. They are usually **short factual questions** about distances, planets, stars, and space terms.

Below is a **high-yield quick revision sheet**.

---

# Important Astronomy Distance Units

| Unit | Meaning | Approx Value |
|---|---|---|
| Astronomical Unit (AU) | Average distance between Earth and Sun | (1.496 × 10^{11}) m |
| Light Year | Distance light travels in one year | (9.46 × 10^{12}) km |
| Parsec | Distance used for stars | **3.26 light years** |

Quick relation:

[
1 \text{ parsec } \approx 3.26 \text{ light years}
]

---

# Important Solar System Facts

| Fact | Answer |
|---|---|
| Closest planet to Sun | Mercury |
| Largest planet | Jupiter |
| Hottest planet | Venus |
| Red planet | Mars |
| Planet with rings | Saturn |
| Largest moon | Ganymede |

---

# Important Star Facts

| Concept | Explanation |
|---|---|
| Nearest star to Earth | Sun |
| Nearest star after Sun | Proxima Centauri |
| Our galaxy | Milky Way |

---

# Important Space Concepts

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Black hole | Region with extremely strong gravity |
| Supernova | Explosion of a massive star |
| Nebula | Cloud of gas and dust in space |
| Asteroid | Small rocky body orbiting the Sun |
| Comet | Ice-dust object with tail near the Sun |

---

# Very Common Exam MCQs

**1. The distance between Earth and Sun is called:**

A. Light year
B. Astronomical unit
C. Parsec
D. Kilometer

✅ **Answer: Astronomical unit**

---

**2. One parsec is approximately equal to:**

A. 1 light year
B. 2 light years
C. 3.26 light years
D. 5 light years

✅ **Answer: 3.26 light years**

---

**3. Which planet is known as the Red Planet?**

A. Venus
B. Earth
C. Mars
D. Jupiter

✅ **Answer: Mars**

---

# 10-Second Memory Tricks

• **AU → Earth–Sun distance**
• **Light year → distance light travels in 1 year**
• **Parsec → 3.26 light years**

---

You can also review **compact one-line facts from Physics, Chemistry, and Biology**, which help cover many **DRDO CEPTAM Tier-1 style questions** quickly.

questions.text: PARSEC is the unit of
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Distance**

questions.text: Which unit of measurement is multiplied by 0.39 to convert it to ‘inches’?
questions.solution:
This question is from **unit conversion (metric system to imperial system)**, which sometimes appears in the General Science section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Which unit of measurement is multiplied by 0.39 to convert it to inches?**

A. Millimetre
B. Centimetre
C. Metre
D. Decimetre

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Centimetre**

---

# Core Concept

The relation between **centimetres and inches** is:

[
1 \text{ inch} = 2.54 \text{ cm}
]

Therefore:

[
1 \text{ cm} \approx 0.3937 \text{ inches}
]

In approximate calculations used in exams:

[
1 \text{ cm} \approx 0.39 \text{ inches}
]

So to convert **centimetres → inches**, multiply by **0.39**.

Example:

[
10 \text{ cm} \times 0.39 = 3.9 \text{ inches}
]

Thus the correct answer is **centimetre**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Millimetre ❌ (Incorrect)

Relation:

[
1 \text{ mm} = 0.039 \text{ inches}
]

Thus mm must be multiplied by **0.039**, not **0.39**.

---

## B. Centimetre ✔ (Correct)

Relation:

[
1 \text{ cm} \approx 0.39 \text{ inches}
]

Thus centimetres are multiplied by **0.39** to convert into inches.

---

## C. Metre ❌ (Incorrect)

Relation:

[
1 \text{ m} = 39.37 \text{ inches}
]

To convert metres to inches, you multiply by **39.37**, not 0.39.

---

## D. Decimetre ❌ (Incorrect)

Relation:

[
1 \text{ dm} = 3.937 \text{ inches}
]

Thus decimetres must be multiplied by **3.937**, not 0.39.

---

# Quick Conversion Table

| Unit | Inches Equivalent |
|---|---|
| 1 mm | 0.039 in |
| 1 cm | **0.39 in** |
| 1 dm | 3.937 in |
| 1 m | 39.37 in |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember:

[
1 \text{ inch} \approx 2.54 \text{ cm}
]

So:

[
\text{cm} \times 0.39 \rightarrow \text{inches}
]

---

You can also review **important unit-conversion shortcuts for length, mass, pressure, and temperature**, which are useful in **DRDO CEPTAM and other competitive examinations**.

questions.text: How can the height of a person who is six feet tall be expressed approximately in nanometre?
questions.solution:
This question tests **unit conversion and order of magnitude**, which sometimes appears in the Physics part of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

We must convert the **height of a person (6 feet)** into **nanometres (nm)**.

---

# Step 1: Convert Feet → Metres

Relation:

[
1 \text{ foot} = 0.3048 \text{ m}
]

So for **6 feet**:

[
6 \times 0.3048 = 1.8288 \text{ m}
]

Approximate value:

[
\approx 1.83 \text{ m}
]

---

# Step 2: Convert Metres → Nanometres

Relation:

[
1 \text{ m} = 10^9 \text{ nm}
]

So:

[
1.83 \text{ m} = 1.83 \times 10^9 \text{ nm}
]

---

# Step 3: Convert into Option Format

[
1.83 \times 10^9
= 183 \times 10^7
]

Thus:

[
183 \times 10^7 \text{ nm}
]

---

# Correct Answer

✅ **C. (183 × 10^7) nm**

---

# Explanation of Each Option

### A. (183 × 10^6) nm ❌

This equals:

[
1.83 \times 10^8 \text{ nm}
]

But actual value is about (1.83 × 10^9) nm, so this is **too small**.

---

### B. (234 × 10^6) nm ❌

This equals:

[
2.34 \times 10^8 \text{ nm}
]

Again **too small** compared with the correct value.

---

### C. (183 × 10^7) nm ✔

This equals:

[
1.83 \times 10^9 \text{ nm}
]

Which matches the converted height.

---

### D. (181 × 10^7) nm ❌

This equals:

[
1.81 \times 10^9 \text{ nm}
]

Close but **not the correct approximation**.

---

# Quick Unit Facts

| Unit | Relation |
|---|---|
| 1 m | (10^9) nm |
| 1 cm | (10^7) nm |
| 1 mm | (10^6) nm |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

For a **human height (~2 m)**:

[
2 \text{ m} \approx 2 \times 10^9 \text{ nm}
]

So any correct option must be around **(10^9) nm**.

That immediately points to **(183 × 10^7)**.

---

You can also review **quick exponent-based unit conversion tricks**, which help solve similar questions very quickly in **DRDO CEPTAM Tier-1 examinations**.

questions.text: A nanometre is equal to
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: 10⁻⁷ cm**

Explanation:
1 nanometre = 10⁻⁹ m = 10⁻⁷ cm.

questions.text: Ampere is the unit of
questions.solution: **Correct Answer: Electric current**

questions.text: Megawatt is the measuring unit of power which is
questions.solution:
This question is from **basic electricity and power units**, which frequently appears in the General Science section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Megawatt is the measuring unit of power which is**

A. Generated
B. Consumed
C. Saved
D. Lost in transmission

✅ **Correct Answer: A. Generated**

---

# Core Concept

**Power** is the rate at which energy is produced or used.

Formula:

[
P = \frac{E}{t}
]

Where
P = power
E = energy
t = time

The SI unit of power is **Watt (W)**.

Common multiples:

| Unit | Value |
|---|---|
| 1 kilowatt (kW) | (10^3) W |
| 1 megawatt (MW) | (10^6) W |
| 1 gigawatt (GW) | (10^9) W |

---

# Why Megawatt is Used

**Megawatt** is used to measure **large amounts of power**, especially in:

• Power plants
• Electrical power generation
• Large industrial systems

Example:

• A power plant may produce **500 MW of electricity**.

Thus **megawatt commonly refers to generated electrical power**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Generated ✔ (Correct)

Electric power stations measure their **power production** in megawatts.

Example:

• A thermal power plant may generate **1000 MW**.

Thus megawatt is commonly used for **generated power**.

---

## B. Consumed ❌ (Incorrect)

Power consumption is usually measured in:

• **kilowatts (kW)**
• **kilowatt-hours (kWh)** for energy usage

Example:

• Household electricity consumption.

Thus this option is not the typical context.

---

## C. Saved ❌ (Incorrect)

Power itself is not saved; **energy** can be stored or saved.

Thus this option is incorrect.

---

## D. Lost in transmission ❌ (Incorrect)

Power losses occur during transmission, but megawatt is **not specifically defined for power loss**.

It is mainly used to indicate **power generation capacity**.

---

# Quick Example

| Power Source | Power Output |
|---|---|
| Small generator | Few kW |
| Large power plant | Hundreds of MW |
| Nuclear plant | Several GW |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember:

• **kW → household appliances**
• **MW → power plant generation**

Thus the correct answer is **Generated**.

questions.text: One micron represents a length of
questions.solution:
This question is from **basic unit conversion in the metric system**, which frequently appears in the Physics section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**One micron represents a length of**

A. $$ 10^{-6} $$ cm
B. $$ 10^{-4} $$ cm
C. 1 mm
D. 1 m

✅ **Correct Answer: B. $$ 10^{-4} $$ cm**

---

# Core Concept

A **micron** is another name for a **micrometre (µm)**.

Definition:
$$ 1 \\text{ micron } = 1 \\text{ micrometre} $$

Relation with metre:
$$ 1 \\mu m = 10^{-6} \\text{ m} $$

Now convert metres to centimetres.

Since:
$$ 1 \\text{ m} = 100 \\text{ cm} = 10^{2} \\text{ cm} $$

Therefore:
$$ 1 \\mu m = 10^{-6} \\text{ m} $$
$$ = 10^{-6} \\times 10^{2} \\text{ cm} $$
$$ = 10^{-4} \\text{ cm} $$

Thus:
$$ 1 \\text{ micron } = 10^{-4} \\text{ cm} $$

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. $$ 10^{-6} $$ cm ❌ (Incorrect)

This would correspond to **much smaller length** than a micron.

Actual micron value is:
$$ 10^{-4} \\text{ cm} $$

---

## B. $$ 10^{-4} $$ cm ✔ (Correct)

As shown in the calculation:
$$ 1 \\mu m = 10^{-4} \\text{ cm} $$

Thus this option is correct.

---

## C. 1 mm ❌ (Incorrect)

Relation:
$$ 1 \\text{ mm} = 1000 \\text{ µm} $$

So a micron is **much smaller than a millimetre**.

---

## D. 1 m ❌ (Incorrect)

A metre is **one million times larger** than a micron.

Thus this option is incorrect.

---

# Important Length Conversions

| Unit | Equivalent |
|---|---|
| 1 m | $$ 10^2 $$ cm |
| 1 cm | $$ 10^{-2} $$ m |
| 1 mm | $$ 10^{-3} $$ m |
| 1 µm (micron) | $$ 10^{-6} $$ m |
| 1 nm | $$ 10^{-9} $$ m |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember this order of small units:
$$ \\text{mm} \\rightarrow 10^{-3} m $$
$$ \\text{µm (micron)} \\rightarrow 10^{-6} m $$
$$ \\text{nm} \\rightarrow 10^{-9} m $$

Thus:
$$ 1 \\text{ micron } = 10^{-4} \\text{ cm} $$

---

You can also review **important metric prefixes such as nano, micro, milli, kilo, mega, and giga**, which help solve many **DRDO CEPTAM and SSC unit conversion questions quickly**.
